title: Measure cytokine and anti-TREM2 effects on myeloid, NK and T cell activity
text: The objective of this research task (1.1) is to evaluate the synergistic effects of pro-inflammatory cytokines in conjunction with the E3C7 anti-TREM2 monoclonal antibody (mAb) on the activation of myeloid, natural killer (NK), and T cells. This will be achieved through a series of in-vitro assays utilizing co-cultured human monocyte-derived macrophages (huMDMs), NK cells, and T cells. The experimental design involves two primary conditions: one where the anti-TREM2 mAb and cytokines are present, and a control condition devoid of either the mAb or cytokines. The assays will be structured to quantify NK and T cell activity, focusing on their proliferative potential and cytokine secretion profiles. Data collection will be conducted using flow cytometry to assess cell proliferation and enzyme-linked immunosorbent assay (ELISA) alongside quantitative polymerase chain reaction (qPCR) for measuring cytokine levels. The analysis framework will involve statistical comparisons between experimental and control groups to determine the significance of the observed effects. This task is designed to be completed within a defined timeframe, ensuring that results are obtained in a systematic manner that allows for reproducibility and validation of findings. The anticipated outcomes include a comprehensive understanding of how the combination of cytokines and anti-TREM2 mAb modulates immune cell activity, which will inform subsequent phases of the research aimed at developing effective immunotherapeutic strategies against tumors. The results will be interpreted in the context of existing literature on immune modulation and TREM2 signaling pathways, contributing to the broader objectives of identifying optimal MiTE candidates for therapeutic applications. This task is integral to the overarching goal of enhancing immune responses in the tumor microenvironment, thereby advancing the field of cancer immunotherapy.
